subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Senate Filibuster: Beneficial or Detrimental to Policy Making
stimulus: There is debate over the use of the Senate filibuster in the national legislative process. Take a position on whether the filibuster is beneficial or detrimental to policy making in the United States Congress.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Article I of the United States Constitution, The Federalist 10, The Federalist 51.)
parts.scoring_notes: Thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. Does NOT need foundational documents in the thesis row — documents are required only in the evidence row.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning — takes a clear position on whether the filibuster is beneficial or detrimental
alternatives.correct_example: The filibuster is detrimental to policy making because it leads to gridlock and prevents elected majorities from enacting the legislation they were chosen to pass.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: The filibuster is beneficial to policy making because it protects minority perspectives and encourages bipartisan compromise before major legislation passes.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: The filibuster is detrimental to American democracy because it makes the work of Congress less efficient and allows a minority of senators to obstruct the will of the majority.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Lawmaking in the Senate benefits from the filibuster because it forces senators to build broad coalitions, resulting in more durable and widely accepted policies.

wrong_examples: There is debate over the use of the Senate filibuster — restates the prompt without taking a position.
The Senate filibuster is often used during the policy making process — descriptive statement with no defensible claim or line of reasoning.
common_traps: Do NOT require foundational documents in the thesis — documents are only required in the evidence row
A 'because' clause with at least one reason constitutes a line of reasoning
The thesis must take a clear position (beneficial OR detrimental) — 'it depends' answers do not earn the point

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: TIERED SCORING: Award the HIGHEST tier earned — 0, 1, 2, or 3 points. Each tier is cumulative. Tiers 2 and 3 require Row A (thesis).

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 1: Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the filibuster and policy making.

required_elements: One piece of relevant evidence — does not need to support the thesis, only be relevant to the topic
alternatives.correct_example: Mentions Federalist 51, Article I of the Constitution, Federalist 10, or a relevant course concept such as cloture, bicameralism, or checks and balances.

wrong_examples: Irrelevant evidence with no connection to the filibuster, policy making, or the Senate.
common_traps: Tier 1 only requires relevance — the evidence does not need to support the thesis or come from a listed document
Award tier 1 even if no thesis was earned

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 2: Uses one specific piece of evidence to support the thesis, OR provides two relevant pieces of evidence. Requires Row A (thesis).

required_elements: One specific piece of evidence that directly supports the thesis, OR two relevant pieces of evidence
alternatives.correct_example: Uses Article I to argue that because the Constitution only mentions specific supermajority requirements (impeachment, veto override, amendments), the filibuster's supermajority requirement was never intended by the framers and is therefore detrimental to their design.

wrong_examples: Mentions evidence that is relevant but does not connect to the thesis — earns tier 1 only.
common_traps: Requires Row A — if no thesis was earned, cap at tier 1
Two pieces of relevant evidence (even if not thesis-supporting) also earn tier 2 as long as Row A is earned

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 3: Uses TWO specific pieces of evidence supporting the thesis, at least one from a listed foundational document. Requires Row A (thesis).

required_elements: Two specific pieces of evidence, both supporting the thesis
At least one must come from Article I, Federalist 10, or Federalist 51
alternatives.correct_example: Uses Federalist 10's discussion of faction control to argue the filibuster prevents majority factions from steamrolling minority interests in policymaking, AND uses Article I's enumeration of only specific supermajority requirements to argue the filibuster goes beyond what the framers intended — supporting a thesis that takes a nuanced position on its effects.

wrong_examples: Two pieces of evidence neither from a listed document — earns tier 2 at most.
One document from the listed set but only one piece of evidence total — earns tier 2 at most.
common_traps: Foundational document must be from the listed documents: Article I, Federalist 10, or Federalist 51
Both pieces of evidence must support the thesis, not just be relevant

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A (thesis) and at least 2 points in Row B (evidence). Must explain HOW or WHY the evidence supports the claim — not just restate it.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Explains the logical connection between evidence and thesis — how or why the evidence supports the argument
Goes beyond restating the evidence or the thesis
alternatives.correct_example: Article I never mentions the filibuster, and instead allows a majority vote to pass legislation in both chambers. Because the framers designed Congress to act on majority will with only specific exceptions, the filibuster is contrary to the original design — which explains why it is detrimental to policy making: it effectively rewrites the Constitution's rules without a formal amendment.

required_elements: Explains the logical connection between evidence and the thesis
alternatives.correct_example: Federalist 10 argues that a large republic controls faction by preventing any one group from dominating. The filibuster serves a similar purpose by requiring that major legislation achieve broader consensus — so rather than allowing a temporary majority faction to ram through controversial policies, the filibuster forces deliberation and compromise, which is beneficial for durable policymaking.

wrong_examples: Simply listing the evidence again without explaining why it supports the argument.
Restating the thesis without showing how the evidence leads to it.
common_traps: Requires Row A AND at least 2 points in Row B — reasoning cannot stand alone
The explanation must go beyond paraphrasing the evidence — it must show the analytical link to the thesis

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A (thesis). Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut, refute, or concede it. Simply identifying an opposing view without responding to it does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes an alternate or opposing perspective
Rebuts, refutes, or concedes the opposing perspective with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: It could be argued that the filibuster is beneficial to policy making because it ensures minority voices are protected and forces consensus. However, this protection can be exploited for partisan obstruction — a minority of senators can block legislation supported by a large majority of Americans and their elected representatives, ultimately making government less responsive and thus detrimental to democratic policy making.

required_elements: Describes an alternate or opposing perspective
Rebuts, refutes, or concedes with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Opponents of the filibuster argue that it prevents the majority party from passing popular bills and causes gridlock. While this is true, the filibuster ensures that major policy changes represent a broader consensus rather than a temporary majority, which produces more stable and widely accepted legislation — making it beneficial for the long-term policy making process.

wrong_examples: Opponents of the filibuster argue that it prevents passage of popular bills — describes an alternate perspective but does not engage with it or respond to it.
Some people say the filibuster is bad for democracy — acknowledges an opposing view without any rebuttal or engagement.
common_traps: Requires Row A — if no thesis was earned, the rebuttal cannot earn the point
Must do MORE than just acknowledge the opposing view — must respond to it with reasoning
